summary: This paper tackles concept omission in multimodal diffusion transformers (MM-DiTs) for text-to-image generation, the failure mode in which specified objects or attributes do not appear in the generated image. The authors first conduct a diagnostic study, applying linear probes to the key vectors of text tokens across layers, attention heads, and denoising timesteps, and find that certain middle-layer heads encode a linearly separable "omission signal" indicating whether a target concept will emerge in the final image. Building on this, they propose Omission Signal Intervention (OSI), a training-free, inference-time method that extracts an omission direction from a probing dataset and adds it to the target concept token's representation at selected heads during the early denoising steps, actively pushing the model to render the otherwise-missing concept. On FLUX.1-Dev and SD3.5-Medium, OSI reduces object omission and improves attribute binding relative to the base models and recent baselines, including in difficult multi-object scenarios, and the authors provide ablations over heads, timesteps, and hyperparameters.
strengths_and_weaknesses:
The paper is built on a clear and well-motivated pipeline that moves from diagnosis to intervention, and this structure is one of its main strengths. The linear-probing analysis is convincing: showing that the key vectors of text tokens in certain middle-layer heads carry a linearly decodable signal of whether a target concept will actually be realized is an insightful and non-obvious finding, and it grounds the subsequent method rather than motivating it heuristically. Turning this observation into a simple, training-free, inference-time intervention is elegant, and the empirical results are strong: OSI improves over the base models and recent baselines on object omission—particularly in harder multi-object settings—and also extends to the more difficult case of attribute binding. The experimental section is thorough, with ablations over heads, timesteps, and hyperparameters, as well as analyses of model-specific behavior (e.g., SD3.5's CLIP text encoder). The writing is generally clean and the paper is easy to follow. The idea itself is original: leveraging the model's own internal "awareness" of a missing concept as a steering signal is a fresh framing that connects interpretability-style probing with practical generation control, and the significance is real given how common concept omission is in modern MM-DiT systems and how lightweight the proposed fix is.

My main reservation concerns the soundness of the mechanistic claim. The probing experiments establish that omission-related information is linearly decodable from text-token representations, but linear decodability does not by itself establish that the extracted direction is the causal mechanism behind concept realization. The results are consistent with the paper's interpretation, but they do not rule out the possibility that OSI functions primarily as a generically useful steering direction rather than specifically amplifying a token-level omission signal; the current framing states this more strongly than the evidence supports, and I would encourage scoping the claims accordingly. A related soundness caveat is the probing label construction: presence labels are derived from the final generated image while the internal representations are taken from intermediate timesteps, so the supervision is only approximately aligned with the representations it is applied to. Excluding early and late timesteps is a reasonable mitigation, but it also underscores that the alignment is imperfect.

Two design choices in OSI would also benefit from more justification. First, there appears to be a distribution mismatch: the intervention is applied over the interval [t_stop, 1], yet the curated dataset used to compute the direction excludes early-timestep samples, so it is unclear whether the direction is appropriate over the full interval. Second, the steering direction is time-insensitive—a single direction averaged over intermediate timesteps is applied at all steps—even though the diagnostic analysis shows decodability is near chance at early timesteps and the broader literature suggests denoising features evolve over time. A comparison against a time-dependent steering direction would clarify whether the time-averaged choice is a principled simplification or a limitation.

Finally, the paper does not characterize the method's boundaries. As a training-free approach, it would be strengthened by an explicit account of failure modes and capability limits, per-task breakdowns, and an analysis of data efficiency—how many curated examples are needed to obtain a reliable direction and how performance scales with that number. It is also not fully clear how far the approach extends beyond objects to more abstract concepts.
key_questions_for_authors:
1. Can you provide evidence that distinguishes OSI amplifying a specific token-level omission signal from acting as a generically useful steering direction (e.g., comparisons against control/random directions or more direct causal interventions)?
2. Why is the intervention applied over the interval [t_stop, 1] when the curated dataset used to compute the direction excludes early-timestep samples? What happens if the interval is restricted to [t_stop, t_early]?
3. Given that decodability is near chance at early timesteps and denoising features are generally believed to evolve over time, why is a single time-averaged steering direction used at all steps, and how does it compare with a time-dependent direction?
4. How many curated examples are needed to obtain a reliable steering direction, and how does performance scale as the number of examples increases or decreases?
5. What are the method's failure modes and capability boundaries across task types (objects vs. attributes vs. more abstract concepts)? Per-task performance breakdowns or representative visualizations would help.
limitations: The paper does not include a dedicated discussion of the method's limitations. Given that OSI is training-free, the authors should characterize its capability boundaries and failure modes—for example, per-task performance breakdowns, behavior on attributes and more abstract concepts beyond objects, and representative failure visualizations—so readers can understand when the intervention is expected to help or hurt.
